Vol. I · No. 04 · 2026
An Essay · Junior Tennis

The Real Cost of Being a Competitive Junior Tennis Player

Tennis looks like a hobby from the outside. From the inside, it is a second job with no teammates and no days off.

By
The Author
Reading time
11 minutes
Filed from
Cary, North Carolina
I. Ten Years

What you build when nobody is watching you build it

I have been playing competitive tennis for ten years. Since I was six years old, I have been at Cary Tennis Park every Monday through Thursday from 4:30 to 6 PM without exception. That is 2,080 hours over ten years. That is 260 weeks of my life spent on the court.

What does four years of competitive junior tennis actually build? The ability to be completely alone in a hard moment and not quit.

Whether that is worth what it costs depends on what you lost to get there. The court does not care what you lost. The court only cares about the next point.

2,080
Hours on the court  ·  A decade, counted from age six
10 yrs
Years of competitive play since age six
260
Weeks of weekday afternoons at the park
4×
Practices per week · Monday through Thursday
II. The Smell of Cary

Rubber, asphalt, sunscreen — the chemistry of arrival

There is a specific smell at Cary Tennis Park at 4:30 in the afternoon. It is the smell of rubber and sun-baked asphalt. It is the smell of sunscreen mixed with sweat. It is the smell that tells your body it is time to go to work.

Walking to the court is a mental switch. You are no longer a high school student. You are a competitor. Everything else shuts off. Your body knows what to do because your body has done it ten thousand times.

The hardest part is the walk back to the car. Sometimes you have won your match. Sometimes you have lost. Either way, you walk that walk alone. Nobody is there to celebrate you. Nobody is there to comfort you. There is just the silence and the smell of rubber and asphalt fading as you get farther away.

III. An Industry

How a backyard game became a 150-year machine

Tennis was patented in England in 1874 and arrived in the United States in 1876. The USTA was founded in 1881. The Kalamazoo Boys' Nationals tournament has been running since 1911. Winners of that tournament get automatic entry to the US Open.

That is real incentive.

What started as a polite lawn sport became a sorting machine — a long, patient apparatus designed to identify and forward the rare survivor. By the time a fourteen-year-old steps onto a sanctioned court, more than a century of infrastructure is already pointing at them.

1874
Lawn tennis patented in England
1876
The game arrives in the United States
1881
The USTA is founded
1911
First Kalamazoo Boys' Nationals — winner earns a US Open berth
IV. The Burnout

A boom in players, and a quieter boom in burnout

There are 27 million tennis players in the United States. That number has increased by 54 percent since 2019.

A 2024 report from the American Academy of Pediatrics documents the rise of specialization and burnout among junior athletes. A 2020 study looked at more than 3,500 pieces of research on the subject. Single-sport specializers burn out higher on all dimensions compared to multi-sport athletes.

That book was written 52 years ago. It is still the truth. The inner game has not changed. Your opponent is yourself. Your mind is the only thing that will beat you. Everything else is just noise.

+54%
Growth in U.S. tennis participation  ·  2019 — 2024
27 M
U.S. players in 2024 — the highest figure on record
3,500+
Studies reviewed in a 2020 meta-analysis of youth athlete burnout
1 sport
Single-sport specializers report higher burnout on every measured dimension
“
The player of the inner game comes to value the art of relaxed concentration above all other skills. The secret to winning any game lies in not trying too hard. W. Timothy Gallwey, The Inner Game of Tennis · 1974
V. Mid-Drill

The two-second decision that nobody else can make

There is a moment mid-drill where your legs are gone and the sun is in your eyes and you have two seconds to decide whether you are going to go or quit. That moment decides everything. Not your ranking. Not your coach. Not your parents.

You decide. And you have to live with that decision forever.

Honestly I am not sure if that is reassuring or depressing. On good days it feels like the most important thing I have ever learned. On bad days it feels like the thing that has taken the most from me.

“
It is not a gift. It comes with work. I might appear locked in, but trust me, there is a storm inside. The biggest battle is always within. Novak Djokovic
VI. The Ledger

A thousand matches. A thousand losses. One question.

Ten years at Cary Tennis Park. 2,080 hours. 260 weeks. One thousand matches. One thousand losses. One thousand moments where I had to decide to go or quit.

What does that actually build?

The ability to be alone. The ability to be uncomfortable. The ability to know exactly what you are made of because you have been tested ten thousand times and you have shown up every single time.

Whether that is worth what it costs depends on what you lost to get there. The court does not care what you lost. The court only cares about the next point.

1,000
Matches played  ·  1,000 chances to quit  ·  1,000 times you didn't

The court does not care what you lost.
The court only cares about the next point.
